content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum


To      :    Buck Delventhal                                Date     :    January 6, 1986
                                                                          Advice No. I-86-019


From    :    FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
             Robert E. Leidigh

Subject :    World Premier of James Bond Film


     You have requested informal assistance pursuant to 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18329(c). The facts are stated in your
attached memoranda, which are incorporated herein by reference.

     Our informal advice is as follows:

     1.   It appears that the 1,000 tickets were given to the
Mayor as a gift and were not accepted by the City in accordance
with the Commission's Stone Opinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 52, No.
77-003

     2.   However, the 890 tickets sold for the benefit of the
Mayor's Youth Fund would appear to be a transfer (donation) of
those tickets to a charitable organization. Consequently, so
long as the Mayor does not take a charitable tax deduction for
these tickets, they are no longer a gift to her and are not
subject to reporting by her.

     3.   The 50 complimentary tickets given back to MGM appear
to have been "returned, unused to the donor" and are also no
longer a gift to the Mayor.

     4.   That leaves the 60 complimentary tickets which the
Mayor either used herself or distributed to others. These are
a gift to her, she exercised control over them and did not
return or donate them in accordance with the requirements of
Section 82028 or the Commission's new regulations 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Sections 18728, et seq. (Copies of relevant regulations
attached.)

     5.   The value of these tickets is the value of the tangible
and intangible benefits received by attendees. Clearly, this
includes the value of viewing the film in question. That can
be ascertained by looking at the price charged at local
theaters when the film opened to the public. To that price

Buck Delventhal
January 6, 1986
Page 2


should be added an increment for the value of the additional
features of the World Premier, including the free drinks, food,
etc.

     As a suggestion, one method of ascertaining the value of
the tickets would be to ask MGM what the cost of the event was
and then divide that sum by 1,000 (the number of attendees).
The pro rata cost may then be used.

     6.  The Mayor should report the value of the gift from MGM
as 60 times the value of the individual tickets.  For instance,
if a value of $15 was assigned pursuant to #5 above, the total
reported value would be $900.  If $10 was assigned, the total
would be $600.

     7.  Public officials with appropriate filing obligations
who were recipients of the gift tickets from the Mayor must
report those tickets if they received a sufficient number to
equal or exceed $50 in value.  (See enclosed copies of gift
regulations.)

REL:plh
Enclosures

CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO                          OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

100 City Hall                                                    GEORGE AGNOST CITY ATTORNEY
400 Van Ness Avenue
San Francisco, California                                        BURKE E. Delventhal
(415) 558-3588                                                   Deputy City Attorney


                              Memorandum


                              October 22, 1985


TO:        BOB LEIDIGH
           Fair Political Practices Commission

FROM:      BURK E. DELVENTHAL
           Deputy City Attorney

SUBJECT:   WORLD PREMIERE OF JAMES BOND FILM


     I have again spoken to the Mayor's Office and obtained
the clarification you needed to fully advise us regarding the World
Premiere of the James Bond film. The City obtained 1000 tickets
from MGM. The City sold 750 tickets at $125 per person. The City
sold another 140 tickets to MGM at $75 per person. These tickets
were sold to MGM at a discount through the courtesy of the Mayor.
There was no prior agreement that the City would sell MGM these
tickets at a discount. In addition, 110 tickets were given out as
complimentary tickets, 50 of them going to MGM with the Mayor
distributing the remaining 60 tickets to supervisors, bridge
district directors, members of her staff, etc. These tickets came
out of the 1000 given to the Mayor by MGM. As with the discounted
tickets that MGM bought, there was no prior agreement that the City
would give to MGM any complimentary tickets. When MGM offered the
tickets for the benefit performance, they offered them to the Mayor
for the charity of her choosing. The Mayor selected the Mayor's
Youth Fund. Finally, MGM did not retain any block of tickets to
sell on the open market. Should you seek any other information,
please do not hesitate to contact me.

BED:vdg

6872D/19

CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO                    OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

106 City Hall                                F P P C        GEORGE AGNOST CITY ATTORNEY
400 Van Ness Avenue
San Francisco, California 94102              SEP 30 8 47 AM '85     BURKE E.
(415) 558-5555                                                       Deputy City Attorney


                            Memorandum


                            September 27, 1985


TO:         BOB LEIDIGH

FROM:       BURKE E. DELVENTHAL [signature]
            Deputy City Attorney

SUBJECT:    WORLD PREMIERE OF JAMES BOND FILM


            I have spoken to the Mayor's Office and obtained the
additional information you needed to fully advise us regarding the
World Premiere of the James Bond film.  The City obtained 1000
tickets from MGM.  The City sold 860 tickets at $125 per person.
The City sold the remaining 140 tickets to MGM at $75 per person.
These tickets were sold to MGM at a discount through the courtesy
of the Mayor.  There was no prior agreement that the City would
sell MGM these tickets at a discount.  In addition, 110 tickets
were given out as complimentary tickets, 50 of them going to MGM
with the Mayor distributing the remaining 60 tickets to
supervisors, bridge district directors, members of her staff, etc.
These tickets came out of the 1000 given to the Mayor by MGM.  As
with the discounted tickets that MGM bought, there was no prior
agreement that the City would give to MGM any complimentary
tickets.  When MGM offered the tickets for the benefit performance,
they offered them to the Mayor for the charity of her choosing.
The Mayor selected the Mayor's Youth Fund.  Finally, MGM did not
retain any block of tickets to sell on the open market.  Should you
seek any other information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

BED:vdg

6872D/4

10/2/65  called Delventhal to inquire re the authorize
         on the fix - he'll get back to me.

10/4/65

sections.conclusion: The Mayor must report the value of 60 complimentary tickets she retained or distributed as a gift, calculated as 60 times the per-ticket value (determined by either the local theater price for the film plus a premium for premiere amenities, or the pro rata cost of the entire event). Public officials receiving distributed tickets must report them if the value equals or exceeds $50. Tickets sold for charity or returned to MGM are not subject to gift reporting.
sections.question_synthetic: What are the gift reporting obligations for a Mayor who received 1,000 complimentary James Bond film premiere tickets from MGM, with some tickets sold for charity, some returned, and some distributed to city officials?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that 60 of the 1,000 tickets constitute reportable gifts to the Mayor, with value determined by market price or pro rata event cost. The 890 tickets sold for the Mayor's Youth Fund and 50 returned to MGM are not reportable gifts. Other recipients must report if their tickets equal or exceed $50 in value.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter with no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers, but clear numbered advice points provided. Document includes two preceding incoming requests from Deputy City Attorney providing factual background. Minor discrepancy in ticket counts between first and second incoming request (860 vs. 750 sold at $125) reconciled by advice letter using 890 figure, suggesting correction. All core information extracted from main advice memorandum dated January 6, 1986.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the gift reporting obligations for a Mayor who received 1,000 complimentary James Bond film premiere tickets from MGM, with some tickets sold for charity, some returned, and some distributed to city officials?

CONCLUSION: The Mayor must report the value of 60 complimentary tickets she retained or distributed as a gift, calculated as 60 times the per-ticket value (determined by either the local theater price for the film plus a premium for premiere amenities, or the pro rata cost of the entire event). Public officials receiving distributed tickets must report them if the value equals or exceeds $50. Tickets sold for charity or returned to MGM are not subject to gift reporting.

It appears that the 1,000 tickets were given to the Mayor as a gift and were not accepted by the City in accordance with the Commission's Stone Opinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 52, No. 77-003 2. Consequently, so long as the Mayor does not take a charitable tax deduction for these tickets, they are no longer a gift to her and are not subject to reporting by her. 3. embedding.summary: The FPPC provided informal advice regarding gift reporting obligations for complimentary James Bond premiere tickets received by the San Francisco Mayor. The advice addresses how to value the tickets and which portions constitute reportable gifts under California political practices law.
